---
title: "Syllabus — Research in Finance"
bibliography: ../../references.bib
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
listing:
  - id: timetable-listing
    contents: lectures/*/slides.qmd
    type: table
    fields: [week, date, title, topics]
    field-display-names:
      week: "Week"
      date: "Date"
      title: "Session"
      topics: "Topics"
    sort: "date asc"
    filter-ui: false
    page-size: 30
---

## Course information

| | |
|---|---|
| **Title** | Research in Finance |
| **Term** | {{< meta semester >}} |
| **Level** | Master / PhD |
| **ECTS** | 6 |
| **Exam number** | {{< meta exam-id >}} |
| **Instructors** | Prof. Dr. Andre Guettler · Oliver Padmaperuma |
| **Contact** | [oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de](mailto:oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de) |
| **Location** | {{< meta default-venue >}} |
| **Time** | {{< meta default-time >}} |
| **Language** | English |

## Course objectives

This course teaches the practical craft of empirical finance research. Students learn to manage and analyze financial data in R, write up results in LaTeX/Overleaf, and critically read finance papers. The course culminates in two graded deliverables: a group problem set replicating a CFTC futures-position study and an individual referee report on a doctoral presentation.

## Learning outcomes

By the end of the term, students will be able to:

1. Set up R/RStudio and use the language fluently for data analysis (vectors, data frames, functions, loops).
2. Import financial data from APIs and CSVs, cleanse, merge, transform, and visualize it with `tidyverse`/`ggplot2`.
3. Apply standard statistical tests (correlation, t-test, Wilcoxon, Shapiro-Wilk, KS) and run linear regressions with fixed effects and clustered standard errors.
4. Produce publication-ready LaTeX tables (`stargazer`) and embed analyses in an Overleaf write-up.
5. Critique an empirical finance paper using a structured referee-report checklist.

## Prerequisites

- Bachelor-level statistics / econometrics.
- Comfort with one statistical software environment is helpful but not required — the course teaches R from the ground up.

## Required materials

- Laptop with R and RStudio installed (instructions given in Lecture 1).
- A free Nasdaq Data Link (Quandl) API key.
- An Overleaf account (uni email).

## Recommended reading

- @wickham2023r4ds — practical foundations for data wrangling and visualisation in R.
- @scheuch2023tidy — a finance-specific application of the tidyverse, freely available online.
- @angrist2009mostly — the standard graduate text on causal inference and empirical strategies in economics.
- @cochrane2005writing — writing tips by John Cochrane (available on Moodle).

## Timetable

::: {#timetable-listing}
:::

> Adding a new lecture folder under `lectures/` automatically updates this table — no manual edits needed.

## Assessment & grading

| Component | Weight | Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Assignment I — Problem Set (group, R + LaTeX) | {{< meta assignment-1-problem-set-weight >}}% | {{< meta assignment-1-problem-set-deadline >}}, midnight |
| Assignment II — Referee Report (group of up to 5) | {{< meta assignment-2-referee-report-weight >}}% | {{< meta assignment-2-referee-report-deadline >}} |

There is **no written final exam**. Registration in `{{< meta registration-system >}}` for exam **{{< meta exam-id >}}** is mandatory by **{{< meta registration-deadline >}}**. Without registration you cannot submit either deliverable.

**Grading scale:** German scale 1.0 (excellent) – 5.0 (fail). 4.0 is passing. Registered students who do not submit receive 5.0.

### Submission rules

- Submit by **email** to [oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de](mailto:oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de) with [andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de](mailto:andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de) in CC.
- Use the subject and file-naming pattern **`RiF2025_ProblemSet_surname1_surname2_...`** (or `_RefereeReport_`).
- If attachment size is an issue, share a cloud link in the email body.

## Policies

### Group work

- Problem set: groups of up to **five** students.
- Referee report: groups of up to **five** students.
- Declare group composition on the cover page.

### Academic integrity

All work must be your group's own; cite all sources. Use of LLMs (e.g., ChatGPT) is permitted **only to refine** writing and ideas — not to generate the substantive content — and must be disclosed.

### Questions

Asking questions **during or immediately after class** is preferred. Administrative matters (study program, exam eligibility, formalities) should go to the registrar's office: [studiensekretariat@uni-ulm.de](mailto:studiensekretariat@uni-ulm.de).

## Contact

- **Prof. Dr. Andre Guettler** — Helmholtzstraße 22, Room 205 · [andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de](mailto:andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de) · +49 731 50 31 030
- **Oliver Padmaperuma** — Helmholtzstraße 22, Room 203 · [oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de](mailto:oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de) · +49 731 50 31 036
- **Moodle** — all announcements, file drops, problem-set release, and grade postings happen on the [course Moodle page](https://moodle.uni-ulm.de/).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
